text: The surgeon begins the procedure by placing ports and positioning the patient in a modified dorsal lithotomy steep Trendelenburg position. The robotic arms are equipped with Maryland bipolar forceps, monopolar scissors, and tenaculum forceps. The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the bladder outlined ahead. Incisions are made lateral to the left and right medial umbilical ligaments, followed by transection of the medial umbilical ligaments and urachus. The space of Retzius is developed using blunt dissection and cautery until the pubic bone is encountered, revealing the bladder and prostate. The Foley catheter is manipulated to identify the bladder neck, and the bladder is opened transversely above the bladder neck using monopolar cautery. The tenaculum forceps retract the anterior bladder wall, and the bladder is opened until the lateral limits of the prostate are reached. Bladder stones are removed using a specimen retrieval bag through the assistant port. Ureteral stents, placed preoperatively due to ureteral obstruction by the prostate, are visible.

The surgeon uses tenaculum forceps to identify the posterior bladder neck and begins dissection between the prostate adenoma and prostatic capsule. Bipolar and monopolar cautery are used to control bleeding while extending the dissection plane circumferentially. The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the capsule. The dissection progresses anteriorly, and a zero bicoral suture is placed on the anterior bladder as a holding stitch, brought through the anterior abdominal wall. The adenoma is nearly free and is placed in a specimen retrieval bag for removal in two pieces.

The surgeon uses 2-0 microsutures to control the pedicles at the five and seven o'clock positions. The urethra is approximated to the trigone of the bladder using a surgical slip knot, with five interrupted 2-0 microsutures placed. The ureteral stents are removed, and a 22 French three-way Foley catheter is inserted. Hemostatic agents are applied as needed. The bladder is closed in two layers using running 2-0 Vicryl sutures. The first layer is demonstrated, and the bladder is filled with 200 cc of normal saline to check for leaks, with none observed. The second layer of closure is completed.
